does: This paper investigates how explicit usability requirements can cause code-generating LLMs to compromise security by silently dropping implicit security constraints. The authors formalize this phenomenon as UPAttack and develop U-SPLOIT, an automated framework that crafts adversarial usability pressures to induce security regressions. The framework operates in three stages: selecting tasks where models initially produce secure code, synthesizing usability pressures across three vectors (Functionality, Implementation, Trade-off) by identifying rewards of insecure alternatives, and verifying security failures through existing test cases and dynamically generated exploit payloads. Experiments across 75 seed scenarios demonstrate that realistic usability-oriented requirements—such as new features, performance constraints, or simplicity demands—can reliably cause code-generating LLMs to produce vulnerable code while appearing to satisfy stated goals.
connects: This work extends prior research on LLM safety and security by identifying a novel attack surface distinct from adversarial prompt injection or jailbreaking. It builds on longstanding literature examining specification gaming and reward hacking in AI systems, where misalignment between stated and implicit objectives leads to unintended behavior. The paper connects to concurrent work on security properties in code generation, building on studies of how LLMs handle vulnerable code patterns and security best practices. It also relates to the broader conversation about implicit versus explicit requirements in software engineering, and how learned models optimize for measurable signals while neglecting unmeasured constraints. The framework synthesizes insights from adversarial machine learning, automated code analysis, and security testing methodologies.
enables: This research opens several future directions for both attack and defense. It motivates the development of security-aware code generation models that maintain robustness under usability pressure, potentially through multi-objective training that explicitly balances security and functionality constraints. The work suggests designing new evaluation frameworks and benchmarks for code-generating LLMs that test security preservation under realistic feature requests and performance requirements. It also points toward exploring how specification writing and requirement engineering practices should adapt when working with LLMs—making security constraints explicit and measurable rather than implicit. Additionally, the UPAttack framework itself provides a testing methodology that software companies could deploy to identify vulnerabilities in their LLM-based development tools before deployment, leading to more robust integration of code generation into development pipelines.
